---
title: "Chapter 7: Single-Agent Interventions"
subtitle: "Inference and Intervention | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Construct **influence diagrams** with decision nodes, chance nodes, and objective nodes
- Compute **expected value** of different intervention options
- Calculate the **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)**
- Understand the difference between **observing** and **intervening** (do-calculus)
- Apply decision analysis to MNH program investment choices
:::


## Chapter Overview

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">Influence<br>Diagrams</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">Expected<br>Value</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #F8A623;">Value of<br>Information</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #E24A3F;">Observing vs.<br>Intervening</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">MNH Decision<br>Analysis</div>
</div>

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**The shift:** Chapters 1–6 focused on *understanding* the system (inference). Now we move to *acting* on it (intervention). The question changes from "What is happening?" to **"What should I do?"**
:::


# From Analysis to Decision {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">The difference between inference and intervention</span>


## Two Modes of Causal Reasoning

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box}
**Inference (Chapters 1–6)**

Observe evidence → update beliefs about unobserved variables

*"District X has high NMR. What is the most likely cause?"*

$$P(\text{Staffing}=\text{Low} \mid \text{NMR}=\text{High})$$
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Intervention (Chapters 7–9)**

Choose an action → predict its consequences

*"If we deploy CPAP machines, how much will NMR decrease?"*

$$P(\text{NMR}=\text{Low} \mid do(\text{CPAP}=\text{Yes}))$$
:::
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Critical distinction:** $P(\text{NMR} \mid \text{CPAP}=\text{Yes})$ ≠ $P(\text{NMR} \mid do(\text{CPAP}=\text{Yes}))$

Observing that a facility has CPAP is different from *giving* a facility CPAP. Observation reflects existing correlations; intervention changes the system.
:::


## Why the Distinction Matters

::: {.example-block}
[MNH Example]{.example-label}

**Observation:** Facilities with CPAP machines have 30% lower NMR.

But these facilities also tend to have more staff, better training, and stronger management. The observed association conflates CPAP's causal effect with these confounders.

**Intervention:** If we *deploy* CPAP to a facility that currently lacks it (changing nothing else), the true causal effect may be a 12% NMR reduction — still valuable, but less than the naive correlation suggests.
:::


# Influence Diagrams {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Adding decisions and objectives to causal models</span>


## Three Node Types in Influence Diagrams

::: {.definition-box}
**Influence Diagram:** A causal model that includes at least one strategic option (decision) and one objective, in addition to the usual chance variables. Also called a *decision network*.
:::

. . .

| Node Type | Shape | Description | MNH Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chance** | Oval | Uncertain variable | NMR, Quality of Care |
| **Decision** | Rectangle | Variable we control | Budget allocation, intervention choice |
| **Objective** | Hexagon | Payoff to optimize | Lives saved, cost-effectiveness |

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Bayesian network** (Ch 2–6) = chance nodes only → inference

**Influence diagram** (Ch 7–9) = chance + decision + objective → optimal decisions
:::


## A Simple Influence Diagram

<table style="width: 85%; margin: 0.5em auto; font-size: 0.55em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect" style="padding: 0.5em 0.7em;">Deploy<br>CPAP?</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow">→</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.5em 0.7em;">Neonatal<br>Outcomes</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow">→</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node hexagon" style="padding: 0.5em 0.7em; border-color: #3E9B6E; color: #3E9B6E;">Lives<br>Saved</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<span class="dag-arrow">↑</span>
</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder" style="padding: 0.5em 0.7em;">Baseline<br>Severity</div>
</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.incremental}
- **Decision node** (rectangle): Deploy CPAP? {Yes, No}
- **Chance node** (oval): Neonatal Outcomes, Baseline Severity
- **Objective node** (hexagon): Lives Saved — the value we want to maximize
- Arrow from Decision to Outcomes = **causal effect** of our intervention
:::


## Information Links

::: {.definition-box}
**Informational Link:** An arrow pointing *into* a decision node, indicating that the decision-maker knows the state of the parent variable when making the decision.
:::

. . .

<table style="width: 80%; margin: 0.4em auto; font-size: 0.55em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.4em 0.6em;">Pilot<br>Results</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow">→</span><br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; color: #666;">(info link)</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect" style="padding: 0.4em 0.6em;">Scale-Up<br>Decision</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow">→</span><br><span style="font-size: 0.7em; color: #666;">(causal link)</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="padding: 0.4em 0.6em;">National<br>Outcomes</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

The program leadership can observe pilot results *before* deciding whether to scale. This information link changes the optimal decision strategy.


# Expected Value Calculation {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Choosing the intervention with the highest expected payoff</span>


## The Expected Value Framework

::: {.definition-box}
**Expected Value (EV):** The probability-weighted average of all possible outcomes for a given decision.

$$EV(\text{Decision}) = \sum_{\text{outcomes}} P(\text{outcome} \mid do(\text{Decision})) \times V(\text{outcome})$$
:::

. . .

**MNH Application: CPAP Deployment Decision**

| Outcome | P(outcome \| do(CPAP=Yes)) | Lives Saved | Weighted |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equipment works, staff trained | 0.50 | 2,400 | 1,200 |
| Equipment works, staff untrained | 0.20 | 800 | 160 |
| Equipment fails | 0.30 | 0 | 0 |
| **Expected Lives Saved** | | | **1,360** |


## Comparing Two Interventions

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box}
**Option A: CPAP Deployment**

| Scenario | P | Lives |
|---|---|---|
| Full success | 0.50 | 2,400 |
| Partial success | 0.20 | 800 |
| Equipment failure | 0.30 | 0 |

**EV = 1,360 lives**

Cost per life: $14,706
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Option B: Workforce Training**

| Scenario | P | Lives |
|---|---|---|
| Full retention | 0.35 | 3,200 |
| Partial retention | 0.45 | 1,600 |
| High attrition | 0.20 | 400 |

**EV = 1,920 lives**

Cost per life: $10,417
:::
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.key-takeaway}
**Option B (Workforce Training) has higher expected lives saved** and better cost-effectiveness. But this assumes we know the probabilities correctly. What if we could get better information first?
:::


## Decision Trees

The expected value calculation follows a **decision tree** structure:

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**The General Solution Procedure (from the book):**

1. Start at the **objective node** (lives saved)
2. For each **chance node** pointing to the objective, compute the expected value by weighting outcomes by their probabilities
3. For each **decision node**, choose the option that maximizes expected value
4. Work backward (**fold back**) through the tree until you reach the first decision
:::

. . .

This "backward induction" or "folding back" procedure is the core algorithm for solving influence diagrams.


## Multi-Stage Decisions

The MNH program faces a **sequential** decision problem:

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;"><strong>Stage 1</strong><br>Run pilot?</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #F8A623;"><strong>Observe</strong><br>Pilot results</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;"><strong>Stage 2</strong><br>Scale up?</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;"><strong>Outcome</strong><br>Lives saved</div>
</div>

. . .

::: {.incremental}
- At Stage 1: Decide whether to run a pilot program ($2M, 6 months)
- Observe: Pilot results (promising / mixed / negative)
- At Stage 2: Decide whether to scale up the full program ($18M)
- The value of the pilot depends on *how much it changes your decision*
:::


# The Value of Information {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">How much should we pay for better data?</span>


## Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

::: {.definition-box}
**EVPI:** The maximum amount a decision-maker should pay to learn the true state of an uncertain variable before deciding.

$$\text{EVPI} = EV(\text{with perfect info}) - EV(\text{without info})$$
:::

. . .

**MNH Example: Should we learn the true attrition rate before investing?**

Without info: Choose Option B (workforce training). EV = 1,920 lives.

. . .

With perfect info about attrition:

| True Attrition | Best Decision | Lives Saved | P(scenario) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low (0.35) | Workforce | 3,200 | 0.35 |
| Medium (0.45) | Workforce | 1,600 | 0.45 |
| High (0.20) | **CPAP** | **1,360** | 0.20 |

$EV(\text{perfect info}) = 0.35 \times 3200 + 0.45 \times 1600 + 0.20 \times 1360 = 2,112$

. . .

$$\text{EVPI} = 2{,}112 - 1{,}920 = 192 \text{ lives (or } \sim\$2M \text{ equivalent)}$$


## Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)

::: {.definition-box}
**EVSI:** The value of *imperfect* information from a test, pilot, or survey. Always: $0 \leq \text{EVSI} \leq \text{EVPI}$.
:::

. . .

**MNH Example: Value of a pilot program**

The pilot costs $2M and provides an imperfect signal about attrition:

::: {.incremental}
- If pilot shows "promising" → P(Low attrition) increases from 0.35 to 0.60
- If pilot shows "mixed" → probabilities stay roughly the same
- If pilot shows "negative" → P(High attrition) increases from 0.20 to 0.55
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Decision Rule:** Run the pilot if EVSI > cost of pilot ($2M equivalent in lives).

The pilot *changes your decision* only when it reveals high attrition (switching from workforce to CPAP). The EVSI is the expected gain from making this better-informed choice.
:::


# Observing vs. Intervening {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">The do-calculus and graph surgery</span>


## The do-Operator

::: {.definition-box}
**do(X = x):** An intervention that *sets* variable X to value x, regardless of X's parents in the causal model. This is different from *observing* X = x.
:::

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box}
**Observing** $P(Y \mid X = x)$

- X takes value x *naturally*
- All causal relationships intact
- Confounders still active
- "Among facilities that *happen to have* CPAP..."
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Intervening** $P(Y \mid do(X = x))$

- X is *forced* to value x
- Incoming arrows to X are severed
- Confounders blocked
- "If we *give* a facility CPAP..."
:::
:::
:::


## Graph Surgery

::: {.callout-box}
**Graph Surgery:** To compute $P(Y \mid do(X=x))$, delete all arrows pointing *into* X in the DAG, then compute probabilities in the modified graph.
:::

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
**Original DAG:**

<table style="font-size: 0.5em; margin: 0 auto;">
<tr><td style="text-align:center;"><div class="dag-node confounder">Resources</div></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:center;"><span class="dag-arrow">↓ &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; ↓</span></td></tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align:center;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="display:inline-block;">CPAP</div>
<span class="dag-arrow">→</span>
<div class="dag-node outcome" style="display:inline-block;">NMR</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

$P(\text{NMR} \mid \text{CPAP})$ includes confounding
:::

::: {.col}
**After Graph Surgery — do(CPAP):**

<table style="font-size: 0.5em; margin: 0 auto;">
<tr><td style="text-align:center;"><div class="dag-node confounder">Resources</div></td></tr>
<tr><td style="text-align:center;"><span style="color:#ccc;">↓ (cut)</span> &nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp; <span class="dag-arrow">↓</span></td></tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align:center;">
<div class="dag-node treatment" style="display:inline-block;">CPAP</div>
<span class="dag-arrow">→</span>
<div class="dag-node outcome" style="display:inline-block;">NMR</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

$P(\text{NMR} \mid do(\text{CPAP}))$ isolates causal effect
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
After surgery, Resources no longer influences CPAP (we *set* it), so the confounder is blocked. The remaining path CPAP → NMR captures the true causal effect.
:::


# MNH Application: The CPAP vs. Workforce Decision {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">A full decision analysis for a large-scale MNH program</span>


## The Decision Context

::: {.callout-box}
**Scenario:** The MNH program has a budget tranche to allocate in a new target country. The program leadership must choose between two strategies:

- **Strategy A:** Deploy CPAP machines and PPH bundles across 15 hospitals
- **Strategy B:** Train and deploy 200 nurse-midwives across the same hospitals
- **Strategy C:** Split — half CPAP + half workforce

Which strategy maximizes expected lives saved?
:::


## Building the Influence Diagram

<table style="width: 95%; margin: 0.4em auto; font-size: 0.45em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Strategy<br>Choice</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node">Equipment<br>Availability</div>
</td>
<td rowspan="3" style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow" style="font-size: 2em;">→</span></td>
<td rowspan="3" style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node">Quality<br>of Care</div>
</td>
<td rowspan="3" style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow" style="font-size: 2em;">→</span></td>
<td rowspan="3" style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node hexagon" style="border-color: #3E9B6E; color: #3E9B6E;">Lives<br>Saved</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;"></td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node">Staffing<br>Adequacy</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.5em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder">Gov't Co-<br>financing</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

**Uncertain variable:** Government co-financing commitment

- High co-financing (P = 0.40): Government absorbs staff, maintains equipment
- Low co-financing (P = 0.60): Sustainability at risk


## Expected Value Analysis

| Strategy | Gov't Support | P | Lives Saved | Weighted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A: CPAP Only** | High | 0.40 | 2,800 | 1,120 |
| | Low | 0.60 | 1,200 | 720 |
| | **Total EV** | | | **1,840** |
| **B: Workforce** | High | 0.40 | 3,600 | 1,440 |
| | Low | 0.60 | 1,000 | 600 |
| | **Total EV** | | | **2,040** |
| **C: Split** | High | 0.40 | 3,100 | 1,240 |
| | Low | 0.60 | 1,400 | 840 |
| | **Total EV** | | | **2,080** |

. . .

::: {.key-takeaway}
**Strategy C (Split) has the highest expected value** at 2,080 lives. It also has the **lowest variance** — performing reasonably well under both co-financing scenarios. Diversification reduces risk.
:::


## Sensitivity Analysis

**At what probability of high co-financing does Strategy B overtake Strategy C?**

. . .

Let $p$ = P(High co-financing):

- $EV(B) = 3600p + 1000(1-p) = 1000 + 2600p$
- $EV(C) = 3100p + 1400(1-p) = 1400 + 1700p$

Setting equal: $1000 + 2600p = 1400 + 1700p$ → $p = 0.44$

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**If P(High co-financing) > 0.44**, Strategy B (Workforce Only) becomes optimal. The program leadership should focus its advocacy efforts on securing government co-financing commitments — this changes the optimal strategy.
:::


# R Workshop: Decision Analysis {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Expected value, EVPI, and graph surgery in R</span>


## R: Expected Value Calculation

```{r}
#| label: expected-value
#| echo: true

# Define the decision problem
strategies <- data.frame(
  strategy = rep(c("CPAP Only", "Workforce", "Split"), each = 2),
  gov_support = rep(c("High", "Low"), 3),
  probability = rep(c(0.40, 0.60), 3),
  lives_saved = c(2800, 1200,   # CPAP
                  3600, 1000,    # Workforce
                  3100, 1400)    # Split
)

# Compute expected value per strategy
library(dplyr)
ev_table <- strategies %>%
  group_by(strategy) %>%
  summarise(
    expected_lives = sum(probability * lives_saved),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(expected_lives))

ev_table
# Split: 2080, Workforce: 2040, CPAP: 1840
```


## R: EVPI Calculation

```{r}
#| label: evpi
#| echo: true

# EVPI = E[max value per scenario] - max E[value]

# Best decision in each scenario
best_per_scenario <- strategies %>%
  group_by(gov_support) %>%
  slice_max(lives_saved) %>%
  ungroup()

# Expected value with perfect information
ev_perfect <- sum(best_per_scenario$probability *
                  best_per_scenario$lives_saved)

# Expected value without information (best overall strategy)
ev_no_info <- max(ev_table$expected_lives)

# EVPI
evpi <- ev_perfect - ev_no_info
cat("EV with perfect info:", ev_perfect, "\n")
cat("EV without info:", ev_no_info, "\n")
cat("EVPI:", evpi, "lives\n")
# EVPI tells us the maximum value of learning government intentions
```


## R: Sensitivity Analysis Visualization

```{r}
#| label: sensitivity
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

library(ggplot2)

# Vary P(High co-financing) from 0 to 1
p_seq <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.01)
sensitivity <- data.frame(
  p = rep(p_seq, 3),
  strategy = rep(c("CPAP Only", "Workforce", "Split"),
                 each = length(p_seq)),
  ev = c(1200 + 1600 * p_seq,   # CPAP
         1000 + 2600 * p_seq,    # Workforce
         1400 + 1700 * p_seq)    # Split
)

ggplot(sensitivity, aes(x = p, y = ev, color = strategy)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.40, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#005CB9", "#83BD00", "#FA7650")) +
  labs(x = "P(High Government Co-financing)",
       y = "Expected Lives Saved",
       title = "Sensitivity Analysis: Strategy Choice",
       subtitle = "Dashed line = current estimate (p = 0.40)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```


## R: Graph Surgery with bnlearn

```{r}
#| label: graph-surgery
#| echo: true

library(bnlearn)

# Original model (with confounding)
original <- model2network(
  "[Resources][CPAP|Resources][NMR|CPAP:Resources]"
)

# After graph surgery: do(CPAP) — remove Resources -> CPAP
intervened <- model2network(
  "[Resources][CPAP][NMR|CPAP:Resources]"
)

# The key difference:
# In 'original': P(NMR | CPAP=Yes) includes confounding via Resources
# In 'intervened': P(NMR | CPAP=Yes) is the true causal effect

# With fitted CPTs, you would query:
# cpquery(fit_original, (NMR == "Low"), (CPAP == "Yes"))
# cpquery(fit_intervened, (NMR == "Low"), (CPAP == "Yes"))
# The second query gives the causal effect
```


## R: Tornado Diagram

```{r}
#| label: tornado
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

# Sensitivity of Split strategy to each parameter
params <- data.frame(
  parameter = c("P(Gov High)", "Lives if High",
                "Lives if Low", "Cost ($M)"),
  low_ev  = c(1840, 1960, 1960, 2080),
  base_ev = c(2080, 2080, 2080, 2080),
  high_ev = c(2320, 2200, 2200, 2080)
)

params$swing <- params$high_ev - params$low_ev
params <- params[order(params$swing, decreasing = TRUE), ]
params$parameter <- factor(params$parameter,
                           levels = rev(params$parameter))

ggplot(params, aes(y = parameter)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = low_ev, xend = high_ev,
                   yend = parameter),
               size = 8, color = "#005CB9", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2080, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Expected Lives Saved", y = "",
       title = "Tornado Diagram: Sensitivity of Split Strategy") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**Influence diagrams** extend causal models with decisions and objectives, enabling optimal intervention analysis.

**Expected value** provides a principled way to compare interventions under uncertainty. **EVPI** tells us the maximum value of better information.

**Observing ≠ Intervening.** The do-operator and graph surgery isolate true causal effects from confounded associations.

**Diversification** (splitting investment) can reduce risk and increase expected value when facing uncertainty.
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 8 — MNH Resource Allocation Under Uncertainty**

We'll apply decision analysis to a full multi-country MNH program:

- Allocating pooled funding across multiple countries
- Sequential decisions: pilot → scale-up
- Government co-financing uncertainty
- The value of waiting vs. the cost of delay
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[Inference and Intervention | Chapter 7: Single-Agent Interventions]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
